blurb: Loose rosettes of pointed, powder-coated leaves that shift between dove-grey, lavender-pink and pale blue depending on the light. Sprawls on lengthening stems and roots from any leaf it drops — nearly indestructible.
size: Rosettes ~3–4 in on lengthening, trailing stems

care.light: The brighter the light, the tighter the rosettes and the stronger the pearly pink-and-blue tones; in shade it greens up, stretches, and sprawls.
care.water: Classic soak-and-dry — a deep drink once the leaves soften slightly and the mix is dry. Water the soil, not over the farina.
care.temperature: Happy at room temperature and surprisingly cold-tolerant for a short spell, but keep it out of hard frost.
care.dormancy: An easy-going grower with no strong dormancy; it just slows in the darkest, coldest weeks — keep it drier then.
care.feeding: A dilute feed once or twice a year is plenty.
care.potting: Repot when it outgrows the pot or gets top-heavy; leggy stems can be beheaded and re-rooted to restart it compact.
care.propagation: Almost too easy — fallen leaves and beheaded rosettes root readily on dry mix.
care.bloom: Star-shaped white-and-red-speckled flowers on arching stalks in spring.
care.troubles: Overwatering rot is about the only way to kill it; mealybugs occasionally hide in the rosette.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic to cats, dogs, and horses — NC State Extension lists ghost plant as non-toxic.

sources.title: Graptopetalum paraguayense (Ghost Plant, Mother of Pearl) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
